stem:
A 22-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic for advice about missed combined oral contraceptive pills. She says 'I forgot two active pills in a row, the ones in the second week of the packet, and we had sex 24 hours after the second missed pill without protection'. She has been on the same monophasic 30 microgram ethinyloestradiol pill for 18 months with no breakthrough bleeding. Her last menstrual period started on day 1 of her current packet 9 days ago.

She has no recent illness, no diarrhoea or vomiting, and is not taking any enzyme-inducing medication. She has not had unprotected sex earlier in the cycle. She wishes to continue the pill but is anxious about pregnancy risk. She has no past history of venous thromboembolism, migraine with aura, or breast cancer. Her BMI is 24 kg/m squared. She asks whether she needs emergency contraception now, when to start her next packet, and how to handle the rest of this packet.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 74, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Well, no abdominal tenderness, no breast lump on screening
data_table.Medications: Monophasic combined oral contraceptive 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol; no enzyme inducers

lead_in: What is the single best management plan?

options.text: Take the most recent missed pill, continue the rest of the packet, and omit the inactive pills at packet end
options.rationale: For 2 missed combined pills in week 2, the user takes the most recent missed pill, continues the rest of the packet, and goes straight from the active pills into the next packet without taking the inactive (placebo) pills. Emergency contraception is NOT required when 7 consecutive active pills have been taken in the week before the missed pills. (Source: RANZCOG; Family Planning Australia)

options.text: Take the most recent missed pill, finish the current packet, and use condoms for the next 7 days as backup
options.rationale: This plan misses the key step of skipping the inactive pills between packets to prevent ovulation; condoms alone do not address the loss of pituitary suppression. (Source: Family Planning Australia)

options.text: Stop the current pill packet immediately, give emergency contraception now, and restart pills next cycle
options.rationale: Stopping the pill removes ongoing contraceptive cover and worsens pregnancy risk; the missed-pill rules continue the packet rather than discarding it. (Source: Family Planning Australia)

options.text: Provide ulipristal acetate emergency contraception, continue current pills, and use condoms for 5 days
options.rationale: Ulipristal is reserved for cases where 7 consecutive active pills have not been taken before the missed pills, or other criteria are met; it is not required for missed pills in week 2 with preceding pill-taking. (Source: AMH; Family Planning Australia)

options.text: Take both missed pills together with the next scheduled pill and continue the inactive pills as planned
options.rationale: Doubling up the missed pill is acceptable, but continuing the inactive pills between packets re-introduces a hormone-free interval and risks ovulation; skip the inactive pills. (Source: Family Planning Australia)

explanation.summary: Combined oral contraceptive missed-pill rules depend on how many pills are missed and where in the cycle. Missing 1 pill at any time: take it as soon as possible and continue. Missing 2 or more in week 1: take the most recent missed pill, continue, use condoms for 7 days, and consider emergency contraception if there was unprotected sex during the hormone-free interval or week 1. Missing 2 or more in week 2: take the most recent missed pill and continue (no emergency contraception needed if 7 consecutive active pills were taken before). Missing 2 or more in week 3: skip the inactive pills and go straight to the next packet. Vomiting within 2 hours or severe diarrhoea is equivalent to missing a pill.
key_points: Missed 2 in week 2 with prior 7 consecutive active pills: no emergency contraception needed
Missed 2 in week 3: skip inactive pills to next packet
Vomiting within 2 hours or severe diarrhoea equates to missed pill
Enzyme inducers reduce efficacy - alternative method recommended
explanation.pearls: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception 1.5 mg is most effective within 72 hours; ulipristal acetate 30 mg extends the window to 120 hours and is more effective at the late end.

subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes diagnosis and insulin initiation
stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 at 28+2 weeks gestation attends your antenatal clinic for review of her oral glucose tolerance test results. She is asymptomatic. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a baby weighing 3.4 kg born at term. Her BMI is 30 kg/m squared and she has no personal history of impaired glucose tolerance.

Her 75 g OGTT results are: fasting venous plasma glucose 5.4 mmol/L (under 5.1), 1-hour 11.4 mmol/L (under 10.0), 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L (under 8.5). Antenatal blood pressure has been normal. The 28-week scan shows estimated foetal weight on the 60th centile, normal amniotic fluid index, and normal umbilical artery Dopplers. She has commenced ADIPS-style structured education with the diabetes educator and lifestyle measures for 2 weeks. A home glucose diary shows fasting glucose values consistently 5.6 to 5.9 mmol/L and 2-hour postprandial values 8.2 to 9.4 mmol/L, despite good adherence to dietary advice.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 148 bpm
data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height 28 cm, no oedema, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT fasting 5.4 mmol/L (under 5.1), 1-hour 11.4 mmol/L (under 10.0), 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L (under 8.5); home glucose diary fasting 5.6-5.9 and postprandial 8.2-9.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures alone for a further 2 weeks before any consideration of pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: After a 1-2 week lifestyle trial with persistent fasting glucose above target and postprandial values above target, pharmacological therapy is indicated; further delay accumulates foetal exposure to hyperglycaemia. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Guideline)

options.text: Commence subcutaneous insulin therapy with isophane (NPH) at night and short-acting insulin with main meals
options.rationale: Insulin is the first-line pharmacological agent for gestational diabetes failing lifestyle measures in Australia. A basal-bolus regimen with isophane at night for fasting hyperglycaemia and rapid-acting insulin with meals for postprandial hyperglycaemia is the AU standard. Metformin can be added or used as an alternative based on patient preference and access. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Commence empirical glibenclamide as oral hypoglycaemic monotherapy without a discussion of insulin alternative
options.rationale: Glibenclamide crosses the placenta and is not recommended as first-line gestational diabetes therapy in Australia; insulin and metformin are preferred. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Guideline)

options.text: Repeat the oral glucose tolerance test in 4 weeks to confirm gestational diabetes before any treatment escalation
options.rationale: GDM is a single-test diagnosis when ADIPS thresholds are met; repeating the OGTT delays foetal protection. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Guideline)

options.text: Plan induction of labour at 37+0 weeks irrespective of current glycaemic control or foetal growth trajectory
options.rationale: Timing of birth in GDM is individualised on glycaemic control, foetal growth, and other obstetric risk; routine 37-week induction is not the AU default for diet-controlled GDM and most insulin-treated cases proceed to 38-39 weeks. (Source: ADIPS Gestational Diabetes Guideline)

explanation.summary: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed by 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks with the ADIPS-WHO thresholds: fasting at or above 5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour at or above 10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L. Lifestyle measures (medical nutrition therapy, exercise, glucose monitoring) are first-line for 1-2 weeks. Insulin is the AU first-line pharmacological therapy with basal-bolus regimens for persistent fasting and postprandial hyperglycaemia. Metformin is an acceptable alternative or adjunct. Timing of birth is individualised on glycaemia, foetal growth, and other obstetric factors.
key_points: ADIPS criteria: fasting 5.1, 1-hour 10.0, 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L
Lifestyle trial 1-2 weeks before pharmacological escalation
Insulin first-line; metformin alternative or adjunct
Avoid glibenclamide in Australia
explanation.pearls: Postnatal 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum is recommended for all gestational diabetes pregnancies to detect persistent dysglycaemia; up to half develop type 2 diabetes within 10 years.

stem:
A 34-year-old G3P2 at 30+5 weeks gestation is brought by ambulance with a 2-hour history of painless bright red fresh vaginal bleeding. She says 'I noticed a gush of blood when I went to the toilet, no warning, no pain'. Estimated blood loss at home was about 200 mL and continues as a steady trickle. She has had two previous lower-segment caesarean births.

On examination she is anxious but talking, with no abdominal pain or tenderness. The uterus is soft and non-irritable. Speculum examination has NOT been performed yet. Foetal heart rate is reassuring with no decelerations on cardiotocography. Her 20-week morphology scan reported a posterior placenta with the lower edge 8 mm from the internal os. A repeat scan at 28 weeks confirmed placental edge covering the internal os (Grade 4 placenta praevia) with hyperechoic placental lacunae and loss of clear zone, raising suspicion of placenta accreta spectrum.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108 regular, BP 108/64 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C, foetal HR 142 bpm reassuring on CTG
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender non-irritable uterus, fresh ongoing trickle visible at vulva, no speculum or digital examination performed
data_table.Investigations: FBC, coags, fibrinogen, group and screen sent; bedside Hb 108 g/L; 28-week scan: placenta covering os (Grade 4 praevia) with lacunae and loss of clear zone

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess the cervix and the cause of the antepartum haemorrhage
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination is contraindicated in suspected or known placenta praevia until placenta praevia has been excluded by imaging; it precipitates catastrophic haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta)

options.text: Admit, secure two large-bore IV cannulae, administer antenatal corticosteroids, and arrange tertiary transfer
options.rationale: Confirmed placenta praevia at 30+5 with active bleeding requires admission, resuscitation, two large-bore IVs, FBC/coags/group and screen, and Rh anti-D if Rh negative. Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart) are given for foetal lung maturation. Magnesium sulfate is considered for neuroprotection if delivery within 24 hours is anticipated under 30 weeks. Transfer to a unit with paediatric ICU and accreta-spectrum surgical expertise is required given the suspected accreta. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta)

options.text: Proceed immediately to emergency caesarean delivery under general anaesthetic regardless of maternal stability
options.rationale: Emergency caesarean is reserved for maternal compromise, non-reassuring foetal status, or torrential haemorrhage; this woman is currently stable and the foetus is reassuring, allowing time for steroids and tertiary transfer. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta)

options.text: Discharge home after bleeding settles with strict bed rest, daily kick chart, and review in 1 week clinic
options.rationale: A first significant antepartum haemorrhage from placenta praevia, particularly with suspected accreta and two prior caesareans, requires inpatient observation and surgical planning rather than outpatient management. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta)

options.text: Commence intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg for uterine tone in suspected placental abruption with bleeding
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in pregnancy outside the third stage; this presentation is also not abruption (which is painful, with a tender uterus and concealed component possible). (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Placenta praevia is placental implantation in the lower uterine segment overlying the internal os. It presents as painless bright red vaginal bleeding, typically in the third trimester. Definitive diagnosis is by transvaginal ultrasound. Two or more prior caesarean births raise placenta accreta spectrum risk substantially; antenatal ultrasound features include lacunae, loss of the retroplacental clear zone, abnormal bladder-uterine interface, and bridging vessels. Management is admission, resuscitation, antenatal corticosteroids 24+0 to 34+6 weeks, magnesium for neuroprotection if under 30+0 weeks within 24 hours of birth, and elective caesarean delivery at 36-37 weeks in a tertiary unit (earlier if recurrent bleeding).
key_points: Painless bright red bleeding - never digital examination until praevia excluded
Two or more prior caesareans raise accreta risk; image with TVUS and MRI
Antenatal corticosteroids 24+0 to 34+6
Elective caesarean 36-37 weeks in tertiary unit
explanation.pearls: Antenatal betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular two doses 24 hours apart reduces neonatal respiratory distress, intraventricular haemorrhage, necrotising enterocolitis, and mortality between 24+0 and 34+6 weeks.

stem:
A 30-year-old G2P1 at 37+0 weeks gestation with a dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twin pregnancy attends your antenatal clinic for delivery planning. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Both foetuses are cephalic on this morning's growth scan, with concordant estimated foetal weights (twin 1 estimated 2.8 kg, twin 2 estimated 2.7 kg, both above the 10th centile). Amniotic fluid volumes are normal in both sacs and umbilical artery Dopplers are normal.

She has no prior caesarean and her previous singleton birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at term. Maternal blood pressure has been normal throughout. There is no diabetes, no cholestasis, and no growth restriction. The booking ultrasound at 11+3 weeks documented dichorionic diamniotic placentation with a lambda sign. She is keen for vaginal birth if safe and asks about timing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C, twin 1 HR 138 bpm, twin 2 HR 144 bpm
data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height 40 cm, both twins cephalic on scan, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: Growth scan today: twin 1 EFW 2.8 kg (>10th), twin 2 EFW 2.7 kg (>10th), normal AFI and umbilical Dopplers both

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for timing of birth?

options.text: Plan elective caesarean delivery at 36+0 weeks gestation regardless of presentation of the twins
options.rationale: DCDA twins with twin 1 cephalic do not routinely require caesarean and 36+0 weeks is earlier than the AU recommended timing of birth for uncomplicated DCDA. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend planned birth at 37+0 to 38+0 weeks gestation by mode appropriate to foetal presentation at the time
options.rationale: Uncomplicated DCDA twins are recommended for planned birth at 37+0 to 38+0 weeks gestation in Australia; continuing pregnancy beyond this raises stillbirth risk relative to comparable singletons. Mode of birth is guided by twin 1 presentation: cephalic twin 1 allows planned vaginal birth (with continuous foetal monitoring of both twins and an obstetrician immediately available for twin 2). (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy; Royal Women's Hospital Vic Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)
source_refs: Royal Women's Hospital Vic Multiple Pregnancy Guideline

options.text: Defer until 40+0 weeks gestation provided cardiotocography, growth, and Dopplers remain normal weekly
options.rationale: DCDA twins beyond 38 weeks have higher stillbirth risk than comparable singletons; routine continuation to 40 weeks is not the AU position. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean delivery at 38+0 weeks gestation because both twins are cephalic and concordant
options.rationale: Cephalic-cephalic concordant uncomplicated DCDA twins are candidates for planned vaginal birth; caesarean is not the default. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth at 39+0 to 40+0 weeks regardless of presentations because the pregnancy has been uneventful
options.rationale: Even uneventful DCDA twin pregnancies are advised birth at 37+0 to 38+0 weeks; the increased stillbirth risk in twin pregnancies past this point is documented in AU and international cohorts. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Twin pregnancy timing of birth depends on chorionicity. Uncomplicated dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA): plan birth 37+0 to 38+0 weeks. Uncomplicated monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA): plan birth 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. Monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA): plan caesarean 32+0 to 34+0 weeks. Mode of birth is guided by twin 1 presentation: cephalic twin 1 is candidate for vaginal birth with continuous monitoring of both foetuses and immediate obstetric availability for twin 2. Twin 2 management options include cephalic-cephalic delivery, breech extraction, or internal podalic version with breech extraction (operator-skill dependent).
key_points: DCDA: birth 37+0 to 38+0 weeks
MCDA: birth 36+0 to 37+0 weeks
MCMA: planned caesarean 32+0 to 34+0 weeks
Cephalic twin 1: planned vaginal birth candidate
explanation.pearls: For cephalic-cephalic DCDA twins, the inter-delivery interval target for twin 2 is under 30 minutes; longer intervals correlate with acidosis and may prompt operative delivery of twin 2.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Vic Multiple Pregnancy Guideline

subtopic_detail: Adnexal mass risk of malignancy assessment
stem:
A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman attends your general gynaecology clinic with 6 weeks of vague bloating and early satiety. She says 'my pants feel tight at the waistband, my appetite is off, and food sits in me for ages'. There is no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no haematuria, and no abdominal pain. Her mother and maternal aunt had breast cancer; she does not know whether either had ovarian cancer. She had a normal age-appropriate Cervical Screening Test 1 year ago.

On examination her abdomen is mildly distended without ascites detectable clinically. Bimanual examination palpates a non-tender right adnexal mass approximately 10 cm in diameter. Pelvic ultrasound performed by the radiology department reports a 9 by 8 by 7 cm right ovarian mass with multilocular solid components, papillary projections more than 7 mm, no acoustic shadowing, and Doppler colour score 4 (very strong vascularity); ascites volume mild. Serum CA-125 is 480 kU/L (under 35) and HE4 is markedly raised. ROMA score is in the high-risk range.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Mildly distended abdomen, mild shifting dullness equivocal, non-tender right adnexal mass approximately 10 cm bimanually
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 9x8x7 cm right ovarian multilocular solid mass with papillary projections >7 mm, Doppler colour score 4, mild ascites; CA-125 480 kU/L (under 35); HE4 raised; ROMA high-risk; FBC and UEC within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently to a gynaecological oncologist for staging laparotomy and intraoperative tumour assessment
options.rationale: A complex adnexal mass with papillary projections, multilocular solid components, Doppler colour score 4, ascites, and raised CA-125 with high-risk ROMA in a postmenopausal woman is high risk for ovarian malignancy. Cancer Council Australia recommends referral to gynaecological oncology for staging laparotomy; outcomes are improved when surgery is performed by a gynaecological oncologist. Image-guided biopsy is generally avoided to prevent peritoneal seeding. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline; RANZCOG)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline

options.text: Arrange image-guided percutaneous biopsy of the ovarian mass before any operative management discussion
options.rationale: Percutaneous biopsy of an ovarian mass risks peritoneal seeding and upstaging; surgical staging is the standard initial approach. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline

options.text: Trial 3 months of cyclical combined oral contraceptive therapy to assess for functional cyst regression
options.rationale: Functional cysts are uncommon in postmenopausal women and are resolved with observation rather than cyclical hormones; this mass is high risk for malignancy. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Repeat pelvic ultrasound and CA-125 measurement in 6 weeks before referring for any specialist opinion
options.rationale: Repeating imaging in a high-risk mass delays diagnosis and definitive surgery; the high-risk findings already warrant gynaecological oncology referral now. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline

options.text: Commence empirical letrozole therapy for presumed oestrogen-driven ovarian neoplasm before surgical evaluation
options.rationale: Letrozole is used in selected hormone-receptor positive ovarian and endometrial cancers, generally adjuvant or for recurrent disease, not as empirical pre-surgical therapy on imaging alone. (Source: eviQ)

explanation.summary: Ovarian malignancy is suspected when an adnexal mass has complex sonographic features (multilocular solid, papillary projections, irregular borders, Doppler colour score 3-4, ascites) and tumour marker elevation (CA-125 in postmenopausal women, HE4, ROMA, IOTA simple rules). Risk assessment integrates clinical, imaging, and biochemical features. High-risk masses are referred to a gynaecological oncologist for staging laparotomy. Genetic testing (BRCA1/2, BRIP1, RAD51C/D, MMR genes) is offered routinely on diagnosis of non-mucinous epithelial ovarian cancer in Australia, with cascade family testing if positive.
key_points: Complex features: papillary projections, Doppler colour 3-4, ascites
Refer to gynaecological oncology for staging laparotomy
Avoid percutaneous biopsy in suspected ovarian malignancy
Routine BRCA1/2 testing on non-mucinous epithelial ovarian cancer diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Even non-familial high-grade serous ovarian cancer at any age warrants BRCA1/2 testing in Australia at diagnosis; up to 15% carry a germline pathogenic variant relevant for PARP inhibitor eligibility.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Ovarian Cancer Guideline

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar at a regional referral hospital. You are called to a labour room at 3 am for a 26-year-old G2P1 at 36+4 weeks gestation. She has spontaneous rupture of membranes 5 minutes ago. The midwife says 'when her waters broke I could see a loop of cord at the introitus, and the heart rate has dropped'. The baby was breech on the morning scan and she had been awaiting external cephalic version this week.

On examination the cord is visible and pulsatile at the introitus. The presenting part is high and not engaged. Cardiotocography shows a sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm without recovery for the past 3 minutes. The cervix is 5 cm dilated. The mother has no epidural in situ and has had no recent oral intake. Theatre is on the same floor and immediately accessible; anaesthetic and paediatric teams have been activated. She is haemodynamically stable. There is no PPH and no bleeding. The cord pH from a small sample is not available.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C, foetal HR sustained 70 bpm on CTG
data_table.Examination: Pulsatile cord visible at introitus, presenting part high, breech presentation, cervix 5 cm dilated, no PPH
data_table.Investigations: Foetal heart trace shows sustained bradycardia; no cord pH available; cross-match pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate single best action?

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part to relieve cord compression and proceed to emergency caesarean delivery now
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with foetal bradycardia is a category 1 (immediate-threat) obstetric emergency. The immediate manoeuvre is manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (using a fist or by filling the bladder with 500-700 mL warmed saline through a catheter that is then clamped) while urgently transferring to theatre for category 1 caesarean. The knee-chest or Trendelenburg position can complement bladder filling. Tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneous) may be considered if delay is anticipated. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse; Royal Women's Vic)

options.text: Attempt manual cord reduction back through the cervix into the uterus and proceed with vaginal breech delivery
options.rationale: Manual cord reduction is not recommended; it traumatises the cord and rarely succeeds. The cervix is not fully dilated and vaginal breech is not feasible. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Wrap the prolapsed cord in warmed saline gauze, attempt vaginal examination repeatedly, and observe in delivery suite
options.rationale: Warming and protecting the cord is reasonable adjunct care, but observation rather than immediate caesarean during sustained bradycardia is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Commence intravenous terbutaline as tocolysis and arrange routine category 2 caesarean within 75 minutes
options.rationale: Foetal bradycardia with cord prolapse is category 1 (decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes), not category 2; tocolysis is a delay-only adjunct, not a substitute for category 1 timing. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Wait 5 minutes for spontaneous heart rate recovery before deciding between vaginal breech and operative delivery
options.rationale: Each minute of sustained foetal bradycardia at the level seen risks irreversible hypoxic injury; delay is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse)

explanation.summary: Umbilical cord prolapse is the descent of the cord through the cervix beyond the presenting part with ruptured membranes, leading to cord compression and foetal hypoxia. Risk factors include malpresentation (breech, transverse), polyhydramnios, prematurity, multiple pregnancy, low-lying placenta, and procedures that destabilise the presenting part (amniotomy with high head, external cephalic version, manual rotation). Diagnosis is by visual inspection of cord at the introitus or palpation on vaginal examination after rupture of membranes with abnormal foetal heart rate. Management is category 1 caesarean while relieving compression by manual elevation, bladder filling, knee-chest position, and tocolysis if delay is unavoidable.
key_points: Manual elevation, bladder filling, knee-chest position to relieve compression
Category 1 caesarean target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
Tocolysis (terbutaline) for unavoidable delay only
Avoid handling the prolapsed cord more than necessary
explanation.pearls: Bladder filling with 500-700 mL of warmed saline through a catheter (then clamped) elevates the presenting part for transfer, comparable to manual elevation and freeing hands for theatre preparation.

stem:
A 45-year-old man has been stable on lithium 1000 mg orally at night for bipolar I disorder for 8 years. He sees you in your general practice clinic for a routine review. He reports good mood stability, no recent episodes, and no relapse since titration in his 30s. He works full-time. He has hypertension treated with perindopril 4 mg daily and has been advised on adequate fluid intake and salt consistency. His lithium-level pattern has been stable at 0.7-0.8 mmol/L every 6 months.

On examination he is well, with no tremor and a normal mental state examination. His thyroid is non-palpable and there is no goitre. He has not been investigated for renal function in the past 12 months because of a missed appointment. Bloods sent today show creatinine 105 micromol/L (60-110), eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73 m squared (over 60), TSH 5.8 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), free T4 13 pmol/L (10-22), serum calcium 2.45 mmol/L (2.10-2.60), serum lithium 0.75 mmol/L (0.6-1.2 therapeutic for maintenance). His last creatinine 14 months ago was 88 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 128/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No tremor, no goitre, normal mental state, well-perfused
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 105 micromol/L (60-110) up from 88, eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73 m squared; TSH 5.8 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), free T4 13 pmol/L (10-22); calcium 2.45 mmol/L (2.10-2.60); lithium 0.75 mmol/L (0.6-1.2)
data_table.Medications: Lithium 1000 mg PO nocte; perindopril 4 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Stop lithium and switch to sodium valproate as the long-term mood stabiliser irrespective of patient preference
options.rationale: Stopping a previously stabilising mood stabiliser carries a high relapse risk in bipolar I; switch decisions are shared with the patient and reserved for clear toxicity or progressive renal injury. (Source: RANZCOG bipolar guideline; RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Recheck thyroid function and renal function in 6 weeks, commence levothyroxine 50 mcg, and document creatinine trend
options.rationale: Subclinical hypothyroidism on lithium with TSH above 4 in a patient who is well requires levothyroxine replacement because lithium-induced hypothyroidism rarely remits spontaneously. The eGFR is stable but the creatinine has risen 17 micromol/L from 12-14 months earlier and should be rechecked in 6 weeks to confirm the trend. Routine monitoring on long-term lithium is 6-monthly lithium level, eGFR, TSH, and calcium. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; AMH)

options.text: Increase lithium dose to 1250 mg orally at night targeting a serum lithium level of 1.0 mmol/L on maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Maintenance lithium targets 0.6-0.8 mmol/L; pushing above 1.0 mmol/L raises adverse effect burden without long-term efficacy benefit and worsens renal risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Discontinue perindopril urgently as it is causing the renal function decline associated with the lithium dose
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors can raise lithium levels by reducing renal clearance, but the lithium level is in range; discontinuation of an antihypertensive without an alternative is inappropriate and the issue is monitoring, not removal. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Reduce lithium dose to 500 mg at night without further investigation and arrange annual lithium-level review
options.rationale: Dose reduction without confirmed indication risks under-treatment and relapse; the level is in therapeutic range. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium monitoring in Australia consists of 6-monthly serum lithium (12-hour trough), eGFR, thyroid function, and calcium. Lithium induces hypothyroidism in 5-30% of patients on long-term therapy (more common in women); levothyroxine replacement is given for symptomatic or biochemically confirmed hypothyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism and hypercalcaemia are reported less commonly. Chronic interstitial nephritis with slowly progressive eGFR decline is the principal renal concern, and lithium continuation versus alternative agent is an individual risk-benefit decision balanced against bipolar relapse risk. Interactions that raise lithium include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazide diuretics, NSAIDs, dehydration, and low salt diets.
key_points: 6-monthly: lithium level, eGFR, TSH, calcium
Lithium-induced hypothyroidism: replace with levothyroxine
Interactions: ACEI, ARB, thiazide, NSAID, dehydration
Renal function decline is slow - assess trend not single value
explanation.pearls: Lithium maintenance level target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L; lithium 12-hour trough is taken 12 hours after the last dose, not at random times, to allow comparable monitoring.

stem:
A 28-year-old man with schizophrenia has been stable on olanzapine 15 mg orally at night for 2 years. He attends your general practice clinic for routine review. He reports good adherence, no relapse, and no recent change in functioning. He gained 12 kg over the first 6 months on olanzapine and a further 4 kg over the past year. He smokes 10 cigarettes a day and is not on lipid or glucose-lowering medication.

On examination his BMI is 33 kg/m squared. Waist circumference is 108 cm. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable. Bloods sent at booking today show fasting glucose 6.7 mmol/L (4.0-6.0), HbA1c 6.4% (under 6.0), triglycerides 2.6 mmol/L (under 1.7), HDL cholesterol 0.9 mmol/L (over 1.0), LDL cholesterol 3.4 mmol/L (under 3.5), total cholesterol 5.8 mmol/L (under 5.5), ALT 38 U/L (under 35), TSH 2.1 mIU/L (0.5-4.0). Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 82, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 33 kg/m^2, waist 108 cm, no goitre, no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 6.7 mmol/L (4.0-6.0), HbA1c 6.4% (under 6.0), triglycerides 2.6 (under 1.7), HDL 0.9 (over 1.0), LDL 3.4 (under 3.5)
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 15 mg PO nocte; nil other regular

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue olanzapine unchanged, refer for dietitian and exercise physiology, and review metabolic profile in 12 months
options.rationale: Lifestyle alone is necessary but insufficient when the metabolic profile meets criteria for the dysmetabolic spectrum; antipsychotic switch and metformin are considered. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Discuss switch from olanzapine to aripiprazole and add metformin for impaired glucose and weight management
options.rationale: Olanzapine is the most metabolically active second-generation antipsychotic. With BMI 33, waist 108 cm, prediabetes-range HbA1c, raised triglycerides, low HDL, and BP 138/86 the patient meets metabolic syndrome criteria. Discussing a switch to a metabolically more favourable agent (aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone) with shared decision-making and adding metformin 500 mg twice daily for olanzapine-induced metabolic disturbance is the AU first-line approach. Lifestyle measures continue. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG; AMH)

options.text: Discontinue olanzapine immediately and substitute clozapine to manage the metabolic and weight gain trajectory
options.rationale: Clozapine carries higher metabolic and haematological risk than olanzapine and is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. It is not appropriate for metabolic side-effect management. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery without trial of antipsychotic switch or metformin therapy first
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery is considered after failed pharmacological and lifestyle interventions in patients with BMI typically above 35 with comorbidity; first-line steps are antipsychotic switch and metformin. (Source: RACGP Obesity Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine to 5 mg at night and accept the increased risk of psychotic relapse for metabolic improvement
options.rationale: Lowering an effective antipsychotic dose risks relapse and is not the AU first-line for metabolic side effects; switch or adjunct metformin is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-induced metabolic syndrome is a major contributor to cardiovascular mortality in schizophrenia. Metabolic monitoring schedule: baseline, then 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months, then annually (weight, waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipid profile, ALT). Olanzapine and clozapine cause the greatest weight gain and dyslipidaemia; aripiprazole, lurasidone, and ziprasidone are weight-neutral or favourable. Management: lifestyle measures, antipsychotic switch when appropriate, metformin for weight and glucose, lipid lowering and antihypertensive therapy by AU CVD risk assessment. Smoking cessation is critical.
key_points: Monitoring: baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months, then annually
Weight-gain hierarchy: olanzapine/clozapine > risperidone/quetiapine > aripiprazole/lurasidone
Metformin 500 mg BD for olanzapine-induced metabolic disturbance
Switch when feasible to weight-neutral agent
explanation.pearls: Metformin 500 mg twice daily added to olanzapine produces a 3-4 kg weight loss compared to placebo at 24 weeks and improves fasting glucose; lifestyle plus metformin is more effective than either alone.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman is brought to your emergency department by ambulance 2 hours after a deliberate overdose of approximately 30 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg. Her partner says 'she had been arguing with her family, then I found the empty bottle and she was already drowsy'. There is no co-ingestion of alcohol or other agents. She has previously been treated for major depressive disorder.

On examination she is drowsy but rousable, GCS 12. Pupils are 5 mm and equal. She has dry mucous membranes, flushed skin, and a soft non-distended abdomen. Cardiovascular auscultation is unremarkable. Bedside electrocardiogram shows sinus tachycardia at 124 bpm with a QRS duration of 130 ms and a prominent terminal R wave in aVR (over 3 mm). Venous blood gas shows pH 7.30, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. Bedside paracetamol level is undetectable. A urinary pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124 regular sinus tachycardia, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 12, dry mucous membranes, flushed skin, mid-dilated pupils, soft abdomen, normal heart sounds
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia QRS 130 ms with terminal R wave aVR >3 mm; VBG pH 7.30 bicarbonate 18 mmol/L lactate 2.4; paracetamol undetectable
data_table.Medications: Amitriptyline 25 mg (regular for depression); ingested approximately 30 tablets

lead_in: What is the single best initial pharmacological step?

options.text: Activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally for gastric decontamination as the single best intervention at this time
options.rationale: Charcoal is considered within 1-2 hours of ingestion in an awake cooperative patient; this patient is drowsy with airway risk and the QRS widening already mandates sodium bicarbonate. (Source: AusPoison Centre TCA Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg boluses targeting QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50-7.55
options.rationale: QRS duration over 100 ms with a terminal R wave in aVR over 3 mm indicates sodium channel blockade by amitriptyline and is the indication for hypertonic sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus, repeated to target QRS narrowing and pH 7.50-7.55. Sodium bicarbonate provides a sodium load and alkalosis that displace the drug from sodium channels. Avoid class 1a, 1c, or 3 antiarrhythmics. (Source: AusPoison Centre TCA Guideline; Toxicology Handbook Australia)

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 5 mg as benzodiazepine for any seizure prophylaxis and supportive care alone otherwise
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines treat established seizures and agitation; they do not address sodium channel blockade and do not prevent the arrhythmic deterioration. (Source: AusPoison Centre TCA Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg increments for reversal of the central nervous system depression from co-ingestion
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose with proconvulsant agents (TCAs) because it lowers the seizure threshold; it also does not reverse TCA toxicity. (Source: AMH; AusPoison Centre)

options.text: Intravenous lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg as first-line lipid rescue for amitriptyline-induced cardiac toxicity
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is a rescue therapy in TCA cardiotoxicity refractory to sodium bicarbonate and supportive measures; it is not the first-line initial step. (Source: AusPoison Centre TCA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes anticholinergic, sodium channel, and alpha-blocker effects: tachycardia, mydriasis, dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, drowsiness, seizures, and dysrhythmias. Sodium channel blockade widens the QRS, causes a terminal R wave in aVR over 3 mm, and predisposes to ventricular dysrhythmias. Treatment is supportive (airway, breathing, circulation) with hypertonic sodium bicarbonate boluses for QRS over 100 ms, hypotension, or dysrhythmia. Activated charcoal within 1-2 hours of ingestion if airway protected; consider lipid emulsion in refractory cardiotoxicity. Avoid class 1a, 1c, and 3 antiarrhythmics. Avoid flumazenil for mixed overdose.
key_points: Sodium channel blockade: QRS over 100 ms, R wave in aVR over 3 mm
Treatment: hypertonic sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg targeting QRS and pH
Avoid class 1a, 1c, 3 antiarrhythmics
Avoid flumazenil in mixed overdose with TCA
explanation.pearls: A terminal R wave in lead aVR over 3 mm at a sinus rate is highly predictive of seizure and ventricular dysrhythmia in TCA overdose and is itself an indication for sodium bicarbonate without waiting for QRS widening.

stem:
A 24-year-old man with a 3-year history of schizophrenia is brought to your emergency department by his sister after 5 days of becoming progressively withdrawn. She says 'he hasn't eaten for two days, he just stares at the wall, and when I tried to move his arm it stayed where I put it'. He has been on risperidone 4 mg orally at night, with intermittent adherence. He had a brief similar episode 12 months ago that resolved spontaneously over 10 days.

On examination he is mute, sitting rigidly upright with his right arm held above his head. He maintains a position into which his arm is passively placed for over 1 minute (waxy flexibility). He grimaces but does not speak or follow commands. There is no tremor. He is afebrile and there is no rigidity beyond catatonic posturing. Bloods show CK 320 U/L (under 200), white cell count 6.4 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0), CRP 4 mg/L (under 5), creatinine 88 micromol/L (60-110), urea normal. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale is 14.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 90, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, waxy flexibility, posturing, no rigidity, afebrile, no tremor, no acute neurological focal signs
data_table.Investigations: CK 320 U/L (under 200), WCC 6.4 x10^9/L, CRP 4 mg/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L; CT head no acute findings; LP not yet performed
data_table.Medications: Risperidone 4 mg PO nocte intermittent adherence

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge and admit for ongoing treatment
options.rationale: Catatonia is diagnosed with two or more DSM-5 features (mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, stupor, mannerism, grimacing among others); a lorazepam challenge of 1-2 mg IV is both diagnostic and therapeutic, with a positive response defined as marked symptomatic improvement within 10-30 minutes. Lorazepam is then continued (typically 1-2 mg three times daily titrated). ECT is the next step for refractory catatonia or malignant catatonia. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Increase risperidone to 6 mg at night and commence benztropine for any extrapyramidal side effect emergence
options.rationale: Antipsychotic dose escalation worsens catatonia and risks malignant catatonia or neuroleptic malignant syndrome; benzodiazepines are first-line. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Commence empirical IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin for any unrecognised encephalitis aetiology behind catatonia
options.rationale: Antibiotic empirical cover is reserved for clinical or biochemical features of infection; no fever, normal inflammatory markers, and normal LP findings remove that indication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Order electroencephalography first and defer any pharmacological treatment until non-convulsive status is excluded
options.rationale: Non-convulsive status is on the differential and EEG is often performed, but lorazepam challenge is diagnostic and therapeutic and can be delivered at the same time without delaying treatment. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Refer immediately for electroconvulsive therapy as the only definitive treatment of catatonia before benzodiazepine trial
options.rationale: ECT is the definitive treatment for catatonia refractory to benzodiazepines or for malignant catatonia; first-line is lorazepam challenge. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a psychomotor syndrome with mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, stupor, negativism, echolalia, echopraxia, stereotypies, and grimacing. It complicates mood disorders, psychotic disorders, autism spectrum, organic causes (encephalitis, status epilepticus, autoimmune limbic encephalitis), and is precipitated by antipsychotic exposure. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale guides diagnosis and monitoring. Treatment is benzodiazepine first (lorazepam 1-2 mg IV challenge); ECT is reserved for refractory or malignant catatonia (catatonia with autonomic instability, fever, rigidity, raised CK). Antipsychotics are reduced or held during the acute phase due to malignant catatonia risk.
key_points: Diagnosis: 2 or more DSM-5 catatonia features
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV challenge is diagnostic and therapeutic
ECT for refractory or malignant catatonia
Reduce or hold antipsychotic during acute catatonia
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (catatonia with hyperthermia, autonomic instability, severe rigidity, raised CK) is clinically indistinguishable from neuroleptic malignant syndrome and is a medical emergency requiring ECT after benzodiazepine challenge fails.
